coredata.dc:title: The influence of AA and AB stacking on the ground state magnetic properties of triangular bilayer graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Magnetism and Magnetic Materials
coredata.dc:creator: Sharifian, Mahmoodreza
Faizabadi, Edris
coredata.dc:description:
Using the tight-binding model, we have studied the impact of AA and AB (Bernal) stacking on eigenstates and eigenvalues of bilayer graphene nanoflakes with triangular geometry. The zero eigenvalues of any single layer nanoflake will be doubled through AB stacking. However, we have noticed that in AA stacking, the eigenvalues are simply achievable through merging two sets of single layer eigenvalues that once added to and once subtracted from inter-layer hopping. Besides, in a single layer within some strict zero energies, the AB stacking still maintains them sublattice-polarized. However, the AA-stacking neglects the sublattice-polarized character of zero states in single layers constituent. Next, we consider onsite electron–electron interaction utilizing single-orbital Hubbard model to show how the different stacking affects the onset of the ground state magnetization in bilayer quantum dots with different stackings. Especially, in AA stacking bilayer, the system always needs a nonzero onsite interaction strength (
U
c
) to be magnetized.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Edge magnetism
Hubbard model
Bilayer graphene
Tight binding model

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Magnetism and Magnetic Materials

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Sublattice definitions and different structures in this work. Blue (red) color corresponds to sublattice
A ( B )
and in figure (c) and (d) the filled (empty) circles indicate atoms in underneath (top) layer. (a) and (b) show AA and AB bilayer QDs with
N
z
= 3
, respectively. (c) and (d) show the top view of figures (a) and (b), respectively. Using empty circle in top-view helps showing the underneath layer through upper layer. Dashed lines highlight the difference between AA and AB stacking.

Ground state energy spectrum and local spins of AA and AB-TBGQD with
N
z
= 3
. (a) and (b) show the local spin densities for AA and AB-stacking, respectively. Blue (red) indicates up (down) spin orientation, meanwhile, radius of objects are proportional to the magnitude of local spin densities. (c) and (d) show the single particle energy states in AA and AB-stacking, respectively. Blue (red) indicates up (down) spin orientation, meanwhile, filled (empty) object coincides with occupied (unoccupied) single particle states.

(a) Critical onsite coulomb interaction ratio
U
c
/
t
interplannar
in AB and AA-TBGQD in terms of triangle size
N
z
. (b), (c) and (d) show spatial distribution of a selected edge state in a single layer, AA and AB-stacking bilayer, respectively. The size of hollow surrounding circles is roughly proportional to the probability of finding an electron on each atom for desired states.

The top panels correspond to SRC and SREC (eigenstates’ spatial distribution). (a), (b) and (c) show edge content of a single layer, AB and AA bilayer of a zigzag triangle with
N
edge
= 4
, respectively. The bottom panels show tight-binding single-particle energy spectrum coinciding with upper panels.

head.ce:title: The influence of AA and AB stacking on the ground state magnetic properties of triangular bilayer graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Iran University of Science and Technology, 1684613114 Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Iran University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics, Iran University of Science and Technology 1684613114, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Using the tight-binding model, we have studied the impact of AA and AB (Bernal) stacking on eigenstates and eigenvalues of bilayer graphene nanoflakes with triangular geometry. The zero eigenvalues of any single layer nanoflake will be doubled through AB stacking. However, we have noticed that in AA stacking, the eigenvalues are simply achievable through merging two sets of single layer eigenvalues that once added to and once subtracted from inter-layer hopping. Besides, in a single layer within some strict zero energies, the AB stacking still maintains them sublattice-polarized. However, the AA-stacking neglects the sublattice-polarized character of zero states in single layers constituent. Next, we consider onsite electron–electron interaction utilizing single-orbital Hubbard model to show how the different stacking affects the onset of the ground state magnetization in bilayer quantum dots with different stackings. Especially, in AA stacking bilayer, the system always needs a nonzero onsite interaction strength (
U
c
) to be magnetized.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanosize carbon allotropes seem essential in future application of high technology devices. Exploiting different physical properties of these allotropes helps to design various fascinating devices. Especially, their magnetic properties appear to have prominent importance in spintronic [1–5]. In fact, the control of electronic spin can be realized in spin-valve, spin-switch, and spin-filtering devices needed in the next-generation spin-based proposal of quantum computers [6,7]. The most famous 2D carbon allotrope is graphene. Recently, the superconducting phase in graphene superlattices has attracted considerable attentions [8,9]. Early articles about graphene also have reported the existence of two, three atomic layers of it [10]. The piling of two layers can be organized in two main AB (Bernal) and AA stacking that both stacking have been synthesized experimentally [11,12]. Mentioned stackings have a crucial impact on mechanical, electronic, and magnetic properties of nanostructures [13,14]. The origin of magnetism in compounds of carbon as a light material is different from other conventional magnetic material like Ni, Fe, and Co [15]. Actually, 2D graphene is nonmagnetic, but its quantum dots (QDs) may exhibit some kind of magnetic behavior near their zigzag edges [16].

A collection of sites is called bipartite if it could be divided into two subsets (say A and B), such that the nearest neighbor of any site in each subset is only from the other subset. If so, in nearest-neighbor approximation, the single orbital tight-binding Hamiltonian of such systems have electron–hole symmetry. Besides, there may exist some zero energy state in the middle of the spectrum due to sublattice frustration in one or both sublattices [17]. Additionally, wave functions of these mid-gap states are localized mainly at the zigzag edges of quantum dots with most affection on the electronic and magnetic properties of system [18]. Meanwhile, these states are distributed on atoms just from one sublattice that also referred to as sublattice polarized states.

Widely used single orbital Hubbard model provides an efficient model describing magnetic properties of graphene nanosize structures [19–23]. Indeed, the results of the mentioned model are in excellent agreement with the outcome of packages based on density functional theory (DFT) method [24,25]. In addition, Lieb’s theorem [26] provides a simple relation predicting the total spin of the ground state in a bipartite system describing by Hubbard model of itinerant electrons at half-filling.

In nearest-neighbor approximation, the AA and AB stacking bilayer graphene also can be viewed as a bipartite graph. Therefore, Lieb’s theorem can be applied to bilayer structures with some cautiousness about sublattice definition while confirming by DFT results [27,25].

The rest of this paper organized as follows. In Section 2, we introduce the selected geometry of bilayers in this work. Also, we review the single orbital Hubbard model and make some definition in tight-binding term to discuss about the magnetic behavior of system. Section 3 presents our numerical results about the minimum onsite energies that triggers the system leading to magnetization. Next, we investigate how the spatial distributions of eigenstates near the Fermi level may change through different stacking and leading to distinct attitude of magnetization. In detail, we will show the eigenvalues and eigenstates of zero modes in a single layer triangular graphene quantum dot, are modified dissimilarly via AA and AB stacking within different consequences on magnetic properties of structure.

The main nano-flakes in this study are AA and AB-stacking triangular bilayer graphene quantum dots (AA and AB-TBGQDs) and the focus is on the triangles with zigzag edges. The number of hexagons in each edge (
N
edge
) characterizes the size of triangle in each layer (Fig. 1 (a)). Furthermore, the imbalance between two involved sublattices in terms of
N
edge
is equal
N
z
=
N
edge
-1 where
N
z
also shows the number of zero energy eigenvalues in the following tight-binding Hamiltonian:
(1)
H
TB
=
∑
i , j
, σ
-
t
ij
c
i , σ
†
c
j , σ
+
c
j , σ
†
c
i , σ
,
where the
c
i , σ
†
and
c
i , σ
are creation and annihilation operators of
π
-symmetry electronic orbitals at site i with spin
σ =
↑ ,
↓
respectively. Additionally, the summation includes all pairs of nearest neighbor sites. Specially, in the bilayer case, the nearest neighbors are both from intraplanar and interplanar atoms.

We continue with an important emphasis on sublattice definitions in AB and AA bilayer structures. Firstly, it is important to note that the AA and AB-stackings are the Crystallographic names. Actually, In AB-stacked bilayer, the sublattice definition in each layer is unchanged and Crystallographic name coincides with the sublattice definition (Fig. 1b or d). Similarly, the AA-stacking Crystallographic name indicates that each atom has a nearest neighbor from opposite layer in the same Crystallographic sublattice (both from A). However, consider a swap between sublattices in top or bottom layer (Fig. 1a or c). So, this appropriate choice of sublattices not only preserves the system bipartite but also make it in accordance with Lieb’s theorem [25]. Eventually, in both stacking, the total spin of the ground state based on Lieb’s theorem reads
(2)
S = (
N
A
-
N
B
) / 2 ,
where
N
A
and
N
B
are the number of sites belonging to each sublattice. Also, Fig. 1 shows the imbalence (
N
A
-
N
B
)
between sublattices for the different AA and AB-stacking in terms of
N
z
(which is also the number of imbalence in each layer). As a result, the AA-stacking of any two similar single layers, always produces a compensated structure (
N
A
=
N
B
)
Fig. 1a or c).

In addition, we have included the Hubbard-term at the mean-field level to incorporate the electron–electron repulsion:
(3)
H C
= U
∑ i
(
n
i , ↑
n
i , ↓
+
n
i , ↓
n
i , ↑
-
n
i , ↑
n
i , ↓
)
,
where U is effective Coulomb interaction strength and
n
i , σ
=
c
i , σ
†
c
i , σ
are the number operators that their expectation values (
n
i , σ
) correspond to the statistical spin-resolved densities. Indeed, mean field average densities are achievable through a self-consistent solution of the total Hamiltonian
H =
H
TB
+
H
C
starting from a random guess for
n
i , σ
s. So, the converged spin resolved densities give local spin densities through
(4)
M
i
= 〈
n
i , ↑
〉 - 〈
n
i , ↓
〉
/ 2 .
In this work, the hopping intergrals for the nearest neighbors in single layer and in-plane bilayer graphene QDs have chosen
t
ij
=
t
in - plane
= 3 ( ev )
[28]. In bilayer cases, for nearest neighbors from opposite layer, the hoppings are
t
ij
=
t
interplannar
= 0.2 ( ev )
and
t
ij
=
t
interplannar
= 0.4 ( ev )
in AA and AB stacking, respectively [28,13]. A fixed
U
in Hubbard model is a more challenging issue [29]. Nonetheless, in this study, the U is a varying parameter to find it’s critical value that initiates the magnetization in a desired structure. The comparison criterion during iteration process is
η =
10
- 10
, where
η = ∣
n
i , σ
new
-
n
i , σ
old
∣
in each iteration. In addition, a minimum onsite energy
U
c
is acceptable if it could bring about at least a local spin density ranging from
M
i
min
= 0.01
to
0.04
.

At the end of this section, we mention to some important details and definitions. In every selected structure, the
H
TB
mixes N localized
P
z
orbitals such that it’s eigenvalues
E
λ
for
λ = 1 , 2 , … N
are the electrons energies in dot. In addition,
∣
ψ
λ
〉
for
λ = 1 , 2 , … N
are corresponding eigenstates belonging to whole structure. In other words, every single-particle eigenstate is a linear combination of the localized
P
z
orbitals
∣
ψ
λ
〉 =
(
1
N
)
1
2
∑
i = 1
N
ξ
i
λ
c
i
†
∣ 0 〉
where square of coefficients
∣
ξ
i
λ
∣
2
indicates the probability of finding an electron in state
λ
(with energy
E
λ
) at site i (also holding normalization condition i.e.
∑
i = 1
N
∣
ξ
i
λ
∣
2
= 1 )
. Therefor, it is possible to find summation of probabilities for any set of sites. For instance, all sites belonging to sublattice A or B, or edge atoms (atoms with two in-plane nearest neighbour atoms). It is also obvious that in
H
TB
there is no difference between up and down electrons, so, we neglect index showing spin of electrons. Finally, we define two quantities in order to study the content of eigenstates in
H
TB
[24].
( I )
sublattice resolved content
( SRC )
of any desired single particle eigenstate ( λ ) ,
SRC α
( λ ) =
∑
p ∈ α
∣
ξ p λ
∣ 2
where p runs over all atoms belong to sublattice
α
= A or B.
( II )
sublattice resolved edge content
SREC
α
of the
λ
th eigenstate,
SREC α
( λ ) =
∑
q ∈ ( edge ∈ α )
∣
ξ q λ
∣ 2
here the summation is only on edge atoms q from a specific sublattice
α
= A or B (atoms with two in-plane nearest neighbour atoms have considered as edge atoms). Using definition
( II )
, we numerically asses that any given state is an edge-state or not. For a certain state, the
SRC
α
s
and
SREC
α
s
are the summation of probabilities for specific collection of sites with different spatial distribution, so, for normalized states their magnitudes are ranging between 0 and 1.

Numerical solution of the Hamiltonian
H =
H
TB
+
H
C
for AA and AB-TBGQD with
N
z
= 3
yields the local spin densities
M
i
and single particle energy states (Fig. 2 ). The total spins are in consistent with Eq. (4) and the coupling between layers is ferromagnetic with total spin
S = 3
(antiferromagnetic with total spin
S = 0
) in AB (AA) bilayer. Moreover, the spin orientation of each atom depends on it’s belonging sublattice (up (down) on A (B)). It should be recalled that energy spectrum and local spins from single orbital Hubbard model bear a striking resemblance to the results of density functional theory (DFT) packages [30,25]. Next, we focus on the critical on-site energy (
U
c
) in which the magnetization appears. Generally, for a compensated-lattice the structure needs a minimum onsite energy (
U
c
) to experience local magnetization. However, for onsite energy U in Hubbard term below
U
c
, all local spin polarization subside to 0. In many cases, the
U
c
s
are size dependent, e.g., in single layer hexagonal quantum dots
U
c
s
decrease by enlarging the size of structure (see Fig. 3 in ref [24]). Afterward, we searched for
U
c
s
while changing the size of triangle in both AA and AB-stacking bilayer. Fig. 3 a shows the
U
c
/
t
in - plane
ratio in terms of the size of triangles. In AB-stacking,
U
c
s
are zero, nevertheless, in AA-stacking they are nearly constant and size independent.

At this point, we try to explain the results in Fig. 3a using definitions
( I )
and
( II )
in
H
TB
, which have been introduced at the end of method section. Consider a single layer triangle with
N
edge
= 4
, and so, a cluster of
N
z
= 3
strictly zero energy states (states 16–18 in Fig. 4 d). For this cluster, the results of
SRC
A
= 1
and
SRC
B
= 0
show a full concentration of states on sublattice A (Fig. 4(a)). Specially, Fig. 3b shows the square of coefficients in state 16 ( ∣
ξ i 16
∣ 2
where i = 1 , 2 , … N ) of the energy spectrum in Fig. 4(d) by exploiting hollow circles around each atom (the size of hollow circles on each atom, roughly show the probability of finding an electron in state 16 on that atom). Interestingly, all coefficients belongs to one sublattice (
SRC
A
= 1
(blue sites)) while the other coefficients are zero (
SRC
B
= 0
(red sites)) that means this zero state is sublattic-polarized (Fig. 4a). Moreover, the
SREC
A
= 0.82
indicates a significant contribution of the edge atoms in these eigenstates (up to percentage 82 in state = 16 and 90 percent in state = 18). However, for the other states (except than zero energy states), the
SRC
A
= 0.5
and
SRC
B
= 0.5
demonstrates equal weight on each sublattice(called extended state); besides, less concentration on edge atoms
SREC < 0.5
. In a similar discussion, Figs. 4e and f show the energy spectrum and Figs. 4b and d show the edge content of AB and AA-stacking of two single layer with
N
z
= 3
, respectively. The AB-stacking, duplicates the imbalance between sublattices
(
N
A
-
N
B
)
, and so the number of zero states reaches to 6 (Fig. 4e). In addition, similar to the single layer, the SRCs of zero energies are sublattice polarized, and the other states are distributed equally on both sublattices (Fig. 4b). Also, see the Fig. 3d that shows the spatial distribution of state 31.

However, the results in AA-stacking is very different. Here, the energies of edge states (double the number of a single layer) symmetrically split in half (states 31–36 in Fig. 4f). Meanwhile, each half cluster experience a shift in energy equal to
t
interplannar
relative to zero in opposite directions (see inset in Fig. 4f). In contrast with a single layer or AB-stacking bilayer, the SRCs in edge state are not sublattice polarized anymore and like extended state have equal weight on both sublattices (states 31–36 in Fig. 4c). But still, the SRECs show the edge character of these states through accumulation mainly on the edge atoms (Fig. 4(c)). Actually, the existence of edge state with energies different from zero, is similar to what happens in a single layer hexagonal quantum dot [24]. However, here, the dispersion of edge states is independent of the triangle size. In other words, increasing
N
z
only changes the number of edge states in each half cluster, (equal to
N
z
) meanwhile, the energy shift remains unaffected (equal to
t
interplannar
). Using numerical calculation, we have noticed that the eigenvalues of any AA-bilayer structure can be obtained from the summation and subtraction of it’s single-layer eigenvalues by the number
t
interplannar
(see the inset of Fig. 4f). The occurrence of mentioned changes in edge content affects the ground-state magnetization in AA-stacking bilayer.

Finally, we study whether and how the edge contents affect the ground state magnetization in previously considered AA and AB stacking bilayer systems. Especially, the focus is on the critical on-site energy, (
U
c
) in which the spin polarization appears (Fig. 3a). In a single layer zigzag terminated triangular QD, the topological frustration in one sublattice leads to the emergence of edge states with strict zero energy. Consequently, By including coulomb interaction through Hubbard term, the half filed zero states can be polarized without any kinetic energy penalty in the limit of
U ⟶ 0
[31]. Since, the AB-stacking duplicates the strict zero energy states (Fig. 4e), the magnetization in AB-stacking triangular bilayer GQDs with zigzag edges emerges from any infinitesimal U (Fig. 3a) with total spin double the number of it’s single layer (Fig. 2b). Generally speaking, if two single layer QD have some strict zero energy state, then AB stacked of them still should have some strict zero energy. As a result, the onset of magnetization in any non-compensated (A
≠
B) AB-stacking QD can start from an infinitesimal U. In AA-stacking and at half filling, the half of edge states with nonzero energy(states 31–33 in Fig. 4(f)) must be doubly occupied with opposite spin directions or with an antiferromagnetic coupling [25] (Fig. 2c). Therefore, the structure needs a minimum
U
c
to be magnetized. Particularly, in triangles, the minimum
U
c
is nearly independent of the triangle size, (Fig. 3a) because, the energies of involved edge states are constant (states 31–33 with energies equal to
-
t
interplannar
) and enlarging the size of structure only changes the number of edge states. In other words, the bigger AA-TBGQDs have more number of edge states in their two half cluster energy spectrum, (Fig. 4f) but at the same time the energies are constant.

In this paper, the appearance of local magnetization in zigzag triangular bilayer GQD with two AA and AB stacking was investigated on the basis of single orbital Hubbard model. Explicitly, we have explored the minimum onsite energy
(
U
c
)
needed to start the magnetization in system. In AB-stacking bilayers, any infinitesimal U, triggers the system and local magnetic moments. Nevertheless, in AA-stacked triangles with different size, the system always need a minimum constant onsite energy
(
U
c
> 0 )
to experience spin polarization. In the tight-binding term, the number of strict zero energy eigenvalues doubles through AB-stacking while the corresponding eigen states remains sublattice polarized. However, the AA-stacking modifies the structure to a compensated bipartite system (equal number of atoms in each sublattice) within some edge state not at energy zero. In addition, we have noticed that the energy spectrum is feasible through adding (subtracting) in out-plane hopping to (from) all eigenvalues of single layer. Using two definitions, we have shown that the edge state in AB-stacking are sublattice polarized while in AA-stacking they find equal weight on both sublattic and through this, the different nature of arising magnetization due to different stacking has been justified.
Mahmoodreza Sharifian: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Edris Faizabadi: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Iran University of Science and Technology (IUST) (Grant No. 160/18402).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum spintronics: engineering and manipulating atom-like spins in semiconductors
Science

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetoelectric devices for spintronics

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetic proximity effect as a pathway to spintronic applications of topological insulators

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Vobornik, U. Manju, J. Fujii, F. Borgatti, P. Torelli, D. Krizmancic, Y.S. Hor, R.J. Cava, G. Panaccione, Magnetic proximity effect as a pathway to spintronic applications of topological insulators, Nano letters 11 (10) (2011) 4079–4082.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: A.O. Leon, J. d’Albuquerque e Castro, J.C. Retamal, A.B. Cahaya, D. Altbir, Manipulation of the rkky exchange by voltages, Physical Review B 100 (2019) 014403. doi:10.1103/PhysRevB.100.014403. https://link.aps.org/doi/10.1103/PhysRevB.100.014403.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Emergence of magnetism in graphene materials and nanostructures
Reports on Progress in Physics

Physical Chemistry Chemical Physics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Mirehi, E. Heidari-Semiromi, Effects of the interplay between electron-electron interaction and intrinsic spin-orbit interaction on the indirect rkky coupling in graphene nanoflakes, Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. 21 (2019) 1324–1335. doi:10.1039/C8CP05041D. https://doi.org/10.1039/C8CP05041D
